A new exhibition at the National Portrait Gallery in London...
articles.content:
Lucian Freud Is Famous for His Unflinching Portraits. These Rarely Seen Drawings Provide an Intimate Window Into His Creative Process
A new exhibition at the National Portrait Gallery in London showcases drawings and etchings from throughout the British artist’s 60-year career
The British artist Lucian Freud is best known for his raw, unflinching portraits. But depending on who you ask, these artworks are either brutally pitiless or intimately genuine. The figurative painter, who died in 2011 at age 88, called himself “a sort of biologist,” interested in “the insides and undersides of things.”
Now, a new exhibition of Freud’s drawings spotlights that attention to detail. “Lucian Freud: Drawing Into Painting,” now on view at the National Portrait Gallery in London, displays 175 of the artist’s drawings, etchings and paintings alongside archival materials. According to a statement from the gallery, the exhibition includes a collection of his childhood drawings, 48 sketchbooks, letters and unfinished paintings.
“For anyone who thinks they know Freud’s work, this exhibition is an opportunity to discover a less familiar, more intimate view,” exhibition co-curator Sarah Howgate tells the Art Newspaper’s Florence Hallett. She adds that the archival materials “reveal the artist’s thought processes in a way finished paintings cannot.”
Freud was born in Berlin in 1922. The artist’s father was architect Ernst Freud, and his grandfather was the famed psychoanalyst Sigmund Freud. When Lucian Freud was 11, his family moved to London to escape the Nazis. After attending art school and serving in the Merchant Navy during World War II, he began working as a full-time artist.
In the 1940s, Freud started making “highly finished linear observational drawings,” per the statement. He shifted his focus to painting in the 1950s. From then on, the artist drew primarily in his private sketchbooks, which now serve as records of his daily life.
Quick fact: What is Lucian Freud best known for?
One of the artist’s most famous works is Reflection (Self-portrait). Created in 1985, the portrait is a raw, unfiltered depiction of Freud, who was in his 60s, in dramatic lighting.“The sketchbooks contain not only drawings but curious details, recurring motifs, telephone numbers ranging from the gas board to the British aristocracy, love letter drafts, betting tips and thoughts on paintings,” Howgate tells Country & Town House’s Olivia Emily.
Freud estimated that during his early career, he made around 200 drawings for each of his paintings. Later, even as painting became his primary pursuit, drawing remained “a way of working out, noticing, getting nearer than near,” writes the London Times’ Laura Freeman.
“The exhibition spans Freud’s entire career,” Howgate tells Country & Town House. “The subjects are quintessentially Freud: intense, unflinching studies of the human figure and condition.”
Howgate says visitors will view finished drawings, informal sketchbook drawings and etchings, a form of printmaking that involves cutting into metal plates, which Freud called a “form of drawing.” Works on display include a crayon drawing he made as a child and an etching of his daughter, Bella, from 1995.
“The drawings parallel the development of his painting style, with the austere linearity of his early work giving way to a more painterly approach, echoed in the pencil and charcoal drawings of the 1970s, and his etchings, which, he said, he wanted ‘to work like paintings,’” Howgate tells the Art Newspaper.
The exhibition shows some of Freud’s finished paintings alongside drawings he made in preparation for them. Howgate tells Country & Town House that by “reuniting” such works, the gallery demonstrates that he drew in order to develop a better understanding of his subjects.
Sitting for Freud was “certainly an experience,” Pat Doherty, the subject of Profile Donegal Man, told the Irish Independent’s Liam Collins in 2023. “The first painting took 100 sittings, the second was 85 sittings and the third sketch was 35 sittings. Each sitting took three hours, so it was a lot of time.”
Viewing the exhibition’s paintings beside the preparatory drawings brought the Guardian’s Jonathan Jones to a sobering conclusion: “I laughed—and not kindly—at Man Posing, a 1985 etching of a nude model on a couch,” he writes. The work is “weird without being witty or moving. Yet a small painting beside it of the same man in the same pose is dazzling, magic.”
Howgate is curating the exhibition alongside David Dawson, the director of the Lucian Freud Archive, which the National Portrait Gallery acquired in 2011. The gallery recently added 12 more pieces from Freud’s estate to its collections, including eight etchings.
Dawson was also Freud’s friend and studio assistant for the last two decades of the artist’s life. He recalled the beginning of his friendship with the artist in a 2018 statement from the publisher Phaidon.
“He always kept the doors closed in every room, but one morning he asked me to come into his studio,” Dawson said. “These large seven-feet-tall paintings of this naked man really stopped me in my tracks. It was just the most intense thing. I thought, there’s no one better at this moment in painting than this.”
“Lucian Freud: Drawing Into Painting” is on view at the National Portrait Gallery in London through May 4, 2026.

articles.title: Jesse Jackson Witnessed Martin Luther King Jr.’s Assassination. Here’s How He Carried the Torch for the Civil Rights Movement Into the Future
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Jesse Jackson Witnessed Martin Luther King Jr.’s Assassination. Here’s How He Carried the Torch for the Civil Rights Movement Into the Future
He emerged as a leader in the 1960s and championed unity...
articles.content:
Jesse Jackson Witnessed Martin Luther King Jr.’s Assassination. Here’s How He Carried the Torch for the Civil Rights Movement Into the Future
He emerged as a leader in the 1960s and championed unity among marginalized groups across the U.S.
Football helped make Jesse Jackson a leader. In 1960, he attended North Carolina A&T State University, a historically Black school in Greensboro, where he competed as a quarterback and became deeply involved in activism.
“My leadership skills came from the athletic arena,” Jackson said in 1984. “In many ways, they were developed from playing quarterback.”
In sports, he told the Washington Post, “you learn how to lose and bounce back … and one of the qualities of leadership is resiliency. You can’t take last week’s tears into this week’s game—and that’s life, too. You must be tough enough to fight, tender enough to cry, human enough to make mistakes, humble enough to admit them, resilient enough to fight back.”
North Carolina A&T students led the legendary 1960 sit-in at Greensboro’s F.W. Woolworth lunch counter, which inspired thousands of students across the United States—including Jackson—to fight for civil rights. In July 1960, while he was at home in South Carolina on summer break, Jackson took part in a sit-in at the segregated Greenville Public Library and was arrested along with seven other student activists. Throughout his time at North Carolina A&T, Jackson remained involved in nonviolent, direct-action protests against segregated public facilities. He graduated in 1964 with a sociology degree.
Jackson would go on to become an American civil and human rights icon; an influential politician and presidential candidate; a diplomat and negotiator on behalf of international political hostages; and a tireless advocate for voting rights, police reform and economic equality. He died on February 17 at 84.
Quick facts: Jesse Jackson’s family
Jackson is survived by his wife of more than 60 years, Jacqueline, as well as his children, Santita, Jesse Jr., Jonathan, Yusef, Jacqueline and Ashley.
Jackson was a son of South Carolina, born in Greenville in 1941. His early life was marked by the challenges of growing up in a segregated society. In a 1988 interview with the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation Jackson reflected on how experiencing discrimination and white supremacy affected him and fueled his activism: “Suffice it to say, I grew up under the laws of apartheid in this country, lived under racial segregation, an environment that fostered low expectations. Something told me I could make a difference.”
In 1965, Jackson began working with the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), led by Martin Luther King Jr. Jackson asked Ralph Abernathy, SCLC’s vice president and King’s closest friend and adviser, for a position in the organization. Although they hardly knew Jackson, King and Abernathy took note of his ambition, organizational skills and leadership qualities, and they decided to take a chance on him. Jackson’s abilities as an orator quickly earned him recognition. He was charged with establishing a Chicago office for SCLC, and in 1966, he led Operation Breadbasket, which aimed to improve economic outlooks for Black Americans. The initiative had begun in 1962 in Atlanta but found its greatest success with Jackson’s iteration, which King called SCLC’s “most spectacularly successful program” in Chicago.
During a 2021 virtual discussion with Jackson hosted by the Smithsonian’s National Museum of African American History and Culture (NMAAHC), biographer David Masciotra, author of I Am Somebody: Why Jesse Jackson Matters, called Operation Breadbasket “the most important but least recognized civil rights story of our history.”
For the initiative, group members obtained employment information about local companies and determined if they underemployed Black people or hired them only for menial or low-wage jobs. Those that did were approached by influential Black ministers, who appealed to them to change their policies. If the companies failed to act, Operation Breadbasket used the organizational strength of Black churches to begin boycotts to deny Black patronage.
According to Stanford University’s Martin Luther King Jr. Research and Education Institute, under Jackson’s leadership, the group “targeted five businesses in the dairy industry. While three companies negotiated to add Black jobs immediately, two complied only after boycotts.” The Chicago branch of the SCLC also took on such companies as local Pepsi and Coca-Cola bottlers and supermarket chains.
In addition to demonstrating Black buying power’s ability to demand better economic opportunity, Operation Breadbasket also offered Jackson a chance to make a name for himself and show off his charisma and eloquence as a speaker. As part of the program, Jackson held Saturday workshops that became a major cultural event in Chicago and beyond, drawing thousands of people to hear him preach in person and on the radio every week throughout 1966. By the following year, Jackson had become the national director of Operation Breadbasket’s programs.
Some members of SCLC, including King, became concerned at times by Jackson’s self-confidence and determination to be a leader, as they felt his personal ambition got in the way of movement goals. Still, Jackson remained part of the organization, and in spring 1968, he traveled to Memphis to join King in support of striking sanitation workers.
On the evening of April 4, as King prepared to leave the Lorraine Motel, he was shot and killed by fugitive James Earl Ray, who fired a rifle from across the street. Jackson was one of the last people to speak with King before the civil rights leader’s death, and for decades he carried the pain and grief of seeing his friend and mentor murdered. Reflecting on that trauma in a 2018 interview with the Guardian, Jackson said, “Every time I think about it, it’s like pulling a scab off a sore. It’s a hurtful, painful thought: that a man of love is killed by hate; that a man of peace should be killed by violence; a man who cared is killed by the careless.”
Buoyed by his belief in the power of a people’s crusade, Jackson said, “We had to make a big decision: allow one bullet to kill a whole movement for which we worked, and forfeit the game, or fight even harder, and we did that.”
After King’s assassination, Jackson continued to carry the movement’s torch. Like King, he was ordained as a Baptist minister. A few years later, in 1971, after increased conflicts with Abernathy, who had become president of SCLC, Jackson formally resigned from the organization. A week later, Jackson founded Operation PUSH (People United to Save Humanity, later altered to People United to Serve Humanity), an organization dedicated to economic empowerment and social justice for African Americans.
PUSH aimed to address issues such as employment discrimination, economic inequality and access to education in Chicago. Jackson continued his Saturday morning rallies and accompanying radio addresses. PUSH raised money from notable Black Americans and gave out awards to prominent Black people in the U.S. and abroad. In leading PUSH, Jackson also began to speak more broadly about issues in the nation that affected poor people irrespective of race. He revived the term “Rainbow Coalition,” a phrase originally associated with Black Panther activist Fred Hampton’s boundary-crossing alliance, adopting it to describe the diverse and inclusive organization that he believed was essential to improve the circumstances of poor and working class Americans.
The vision and tactics Jackson developed in the 1970s and early ’80s, during his leadership of Operation PUSH, defined his legacy. He saw informal segregation, inequity in educational resources, and the decline in stable and well-paying manufacturing jobs that had been key to upward mobility, and he hoped to bring his Rainbow Coalition together to solve these problems. He wanted to focus on the deep, systemic issues that hurt the nation.
As Jackson said at the 2021 NMAAHC event, “If you had a ten-story building and the roof blew off, only the penthouse would be upset, but if the foundation shook, everybody would be in trouble.”
By the early ’80s, Jackson saw racial divisions increasing at a time when he believed diverse collaboration was necessary to counter President Ronald Reagan’s economic agenda. Jackson thought that Reaganomics’ widespread tax cuts, which favored corporations and the wealthy, as well as economic deregulation and a massive decrease in federal spending on social programs, adversely affected most Americans. In response, Jackson resigned from Operation PUSH in 1983 and entered the race for the Democratic nomination for president in 1984.
Jackson’s campaign was revolutionary. He assembled a diverse alliance of supporters and empowered marginalized communities across the nation. At the time, Jackson’s presidential bid was considered the most prominent campaign by a Black candidate since Shirley Chisholm’s in 1972. Jackson finished third for the nomination, behind Vice President Walter Mondale and Senator Gary Hart.
In the speech he delivered at the 1984 Democratic National Convention, Jackson described the community he sought to bring together:
America is not like a blanket—one piece of unbroken cloth, the same color, the same texture, the same size. America is more like a quilt—many patches, many pieces, many colors, many sizes, all woven and held together by a common thread. The white, the Hispanic, the Black, the Arab, the Jew, the woman, the Native American, the small farmer, the businessperson, the environmentalist, the peace activist, the young, the old, the lesbian, the gay and the disabled make up the American quilt. Even in our fractured state, all of us count and all of us fit somewhere.
He also offered an apology, addressing comments he’d made that were widely viewed as antisemitic:
If, in my low moments, in word, deed or attitude, through some error of temper, taste or tone, I have caused anyone discomfort, created pain or revived someone’s fears, that was not my truest self. If there were occasions when my grape turned into a raisin and my joy bell lost its resonance, please forgive me. … I am not a perfect servant. I am a public servant doing my best against the odds. As I develop and serve, be patient. God is not finished with me yet.
Jackson launched a second bid for the Democratic nomination in 1988, which was even more successful due to his increased name recognition and ability to organize and raise funds. Despite his defeat by Massachusetts Governor Michael Dukakis, Jackson’s campaign proved that a Black candidate could garner the wide appeal necessary to win the nomination and eventually the presidency. Twenty years later, Senator Barack Obama would realize that dream.
Jackson’s charisma was on display throughout his campaigns. He even hosted “Saturday Night Live” on October 20, 1984.
In one sketch, titled “The Question Is Moot,” Jackson hosted a game show but declared all of the questions irrelevant because of the country’s problems. As a young Julia Louis-Dreyfus prepares to answer the question “What president is on the $100 bill?” Jackson abruptly interrupts her and says, “Sorry, the question is moot. Under the last four years of the Reagan administration, very few people got to see a $100 bill.”
In 1996, the National Rainbow Coalition merged with PUSH to form the Rainbow PUSH Coalition. With the organization and his platform, Jackson remained a vocal advocate for peace and human rights on both domestic and international fronts. He engaged in diplomacy, negotiating the releases of American hostages on foreign soil and contributing to conflict resolution efforts in various global hotspots. He was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Bill Clinton in 2000.
In his later years, Jackson remained active in public life, addressing issues such as poverty, racial injustice and health care disparities. Even after Jackson announced in 2017 that he had Parkinson’s disease, his voice continued to resonate as he championed causes aimed at uplifting marginalized communities and fostering unity.
Jackson was moved when he visited NMAAHC during its construction and again on its opening day, in 2016. “We’ve gone from the hull of ships to the White House,” he said. “What a journey.” Jackson added that he wished “Dr. King and Fannie Lou Hamer were here, for just a moment in time, to see the fruit of their labors.”
During his 1988 speech at the Democratic National Convention, Jackson shared one of his most enduring messages, three words that represent everything he stood for: “Hold your head high, stick your chest out. You can make it. It gets dark sometimes, but the morning comes. … Keep hope alive.”

articles.title: What Is Skimo? The Newest Olympic Sport Has a Long History in Europe
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
What Is Skimo? The Newest Olympic Sport Has a Long History in Europe
With roots in military training, high-endurance ski mountaineering is finally catching on in the United States
On February 19, the...
articles.content:
What Is Skimo? The Newest Olympic Sport Has a Long History in Europe
With roots in military training, high-endurance ski mountaineering is finally catching on in the United States
On February 19, the world will witness the tactical efficiency of 36 athletes going for gold in the only new Olympic sport at the 2026 Milan Cortina Winter Olympics. Ski mountaineering, or “skimo,” requires intense conditioning and, in some cases, superhuman natural ability, as demonstrated by U.S. Olympic skimo racer Anna Gibson, of Jackson Hole, Wyoming, a professional runner who didn’t start competing in the winter sport until last December.
Gibson and her mixed relay partner, five-time North American and 11-time U.S. national skimo champion Cam Smith, will be the first Team USA athletes ever—and the only ones this year—to explode through the gates in the women’s sprint and the men’s sprint, respectively. And in the mixed-gender skimo relay, the duo will compete against 17 other teams of two, speed-skiing uphill and racing back down in a combination of traditional alpine and cross-country.
But despite an undeniable resurgence of uphill skiing in the U.S. since the pandemic, skimo isn’t new at all, actually. It’s one of the first modes of human transportation, dating back thousands of years.
“Before the advent of ski tows and lifts in the 1930s, any downhill skiing involved climbing uphill,” says Jeff Leich, ski historian and former executive director of the New England Ski Museum in North Conway, New Hampshire. As Norwegian glacial archaeologist Julian Post-Melbye explains, “The oldest piece of wood-claimed fragments from a ski dates back to 6000 B.C.E. Siberians.”
The history of skimo is found everywhere across the high-alpine world. This endurance sport—in which athletes attach removable fabric or “skins” to the bottom of their skis for traction and clip into bindings with detachable heels to race uphill, then pull the skins off to ski downhill and sometimes transition to hiking with their skis on a backpack—evolved from European hunting, exploration and military patrol.
“Italy is the perfect place for the debut of skimo in the Olympics due to the rich history of the sport in the area, which I know is not a coincidence,” says Gibson, who made the 2024 1500-meter semifinal in track and field at the U.S. Olympic Trials, medaled in the uphill 6-kilometer race at the World Mountain and Trail Running Championships, and took state titles in high school track, cross-country and Nordic skiing at her high school, where she was also valedictorian.
Skiing through history
From skiing as a kid at Maine’s Sunday River to now, spending all winter either skiing or thinking about skiing—or reporting on skiing—I am, it’s safe to say, obsessed. So naturally, traveling across the world, I’ve pieced together the story of ski mountaineering. Before they invented the wheel and even domesticated horses, humans were putting sleds on their feet. Mountain dwellers wouldn’t have survived without these wooden planks, depicted in 10,000-year-old prehistoric rock paintings and used for hunting across the Altai Mountains, where this past September I found myself among some of Mongolia’s last nomads, whose ancestors likely invented the ski.
The previous August, I stood with Post-Melbye after his latest excavation in Norway’s Jotunheimen Mountains, where, in 2021, he’d discovered a 1,300-year-old ski—one of the earliest intact—frozen in a patch of ice. “We saw the tip of the ski protruding from the ice,” he told me. “Even as seasoned archaeologists of the mountains of Norway, we were left speechless.”
How to watch: Skimo at the 2026 Winter Olympics
- The men's sprint and women's sprint events are scheduled for February 19, while the mixed relay will take place on February 21. All races will happen at the Stelvio Ski Center in Bormio, Italy.
Long before people were alpine or cross-country skiing for recreation, they were ski mountaineering out of necessity. The Norwegian Viking king Haakon Haraldsson in 950 C.E. sent tax collectors out on skis, and skis carried soldiers through mountain combat in Norway’s 1161 Battle of Oslo and the 19th-century Napoleonic Wars.
In the latter half of the 1800s, a carpenter named Sondre Norheim from Telemark, Norway, whittled the first curved, more maneuverable skis. His telemark skis were worn by another famous Norwegian from Telemark, John “Snowshoe” Thompson, for 90 miles over the steep Sierra Nevada to deliver mail to remote California mining camps and settlements in 1855, bringing skis to the U.S.
The evolution of the sport was as clear as the 2024 bluebird day that I visited the world’s first ski resort, in Davos, Switzerland, which opened for hiking up and skiing down in the late 1800s. On the roof of Europe’s highest and oldest weather observatory, shivering at 10,000 feet in a foot of snow, scientist Elke Ludewig told me about the impact climate change is having on Austria’s Hoher Sonnblick mountain, where German Wilhelm von Arlt, "the father of ski mountaineering," made the world’s first ski ascent in 1894. And riding a chairlift in St. Anton, Austria, I passed over the childhood home of Austrian Army World War I ski instructor Hannes Schneider, “the father of modern skiing” whose Alberg-trained ski mountaineering students opened the earliest U.S. ski resorts before the invention of the rope tow and chairlift.
But not until I was in the Dolomites, first last February and again in December, did I begin to realize how significant Northern Italy is to ski mountaineering. Skiing past deep networks of tunnels inside towering mountain spires and glaciers—where Austro-Hungarian and Italian troops lived and fought on skis during the “White War” battles of World War I, adapting to freezing temperatures at 12,000 feet—took my breath away. Trying to imagine what life was like for the local farmers and mountaineers who became soldiers was mind-bending as I stared at the preserved pickaxes and pulleys they used to chisel tunnels inside dolomite rock to keep themselves alive in alpine warfare now on display at Europe’s highest museum. After visiting the Museum of the Great War on Marmolada Glacier, I skied the scenic Sellaronda ski route.
“A significant part of the history of the Dolomites is linked to the First World War, and this is something many people don’t expect or fully realize when they come here,” says Agustina Lagos Marmol, founder and managing director of Dolomite Mountains tours, which offers the Great War Ski Tour, consisting of 50 miles of war trenches in Italian, German and Ladin (the oldest Latin-speaking people) communities within Alta Badia and Cortina d’Ampezzo. “It’s amazing, skiing and stopping on the side of a slope to see caves and shelters carved into the rock during World War I. It’s an important part of European history and particularly meaningful for international visitors to encounter it in such a direct, physical way.”
Ski mountaineering continued into the battles of World War II. Last February, at the bottom of Vail Ski Resort in Colorado, I watched the reactivated 10th Mountain Division hike up and ski down Vail’s original trail, Riva Ridge. Veteran Pete Seibert (with Colorado rancher Earl Eaton) made the famous 1962 climb up to found Vail, naming its first trail after the Riva Ridge slope in Italy, where his division defeated the Germans in their legendary surprise attack in 1945. These troops, cherry-picked from the country’s top universities to train at Colorado’s Camp Hale, near Aspen, were instrumental in the growth of U.S. recreational skiing, having gone on to open dozens of U.S. ski resorts modeled after the European mountaineering slopes they’d skied.
The roots of skimo racing
Military ski training and patrolling exercises in Europe gradually gave rise to ski mountaineering competitions. At the 1924 Chamonix Winter Olympics, the inaugural “Military Patrol” event tasked teams of alpine troops with raising their rifles on the slopes—a precursor to Olympic biathlon that premiered in 1960 in Squaw Valley at what is now Palisades Tahoe. The ski mountaineering competition circuit took off in the Italian Alps with what’s still one of the world’s most prestigious and grueling long-distance races, the Trofeo Mezzalama. The first 1933 race in Cervinia in the Aosta Valley sent 42 skiers roped into teams of three traversing 28 miles over glaciers and 13,000-foot peaks to honor military patrol traditions. That was followed by Switzerland's Patrouille des Glaciers, where 18 military patrols skinned up and skied down 39 high-altitude miles from Zermatt to Verbier.
With the exception of the iconic Silver Skis ski mountaineering race at Mount Rainier in 1934, where skiers raced up a 5,400-foot elevation gain to 10,000 feet, skimo didn’t become a true spectator sport beyond the Alps until the late 1990s. The first World Championships were held in 2002 in the French Alps at Serre Chevalier. Five years later, the sport’s governing body, the International Ski Mountaineering Federation, was formed. Skimo’s inclusion in the 2020 Winter Youth Olympic Games in Lausanne, Switzerland, where male and female skiers competed in 4.5-mile-long individual, 230-foot-climb sprint, and 1,000-foot-ascent mixed relay events, was enough to persuade the Olympic Committee in 2021 to name it an official medal sport in the 2026 Olympic Winter Games.
Despite shared roots and form, skimo the Olympic sport still looks very different from the uphill off-piste skiing, or “alpine touring,” that proliferated post-pandemic in the backcountry of U.S. ski resorts as a cheaper, crowdless alternative to resort skiing. Both involve sticking skins to the bottom of lightweight skis paired with boots that unlock in the heel to gain traction skinning uphill, a technique first achieved using animal hide at least as far back as 1555 in Scandinavia. But the abridged Olympic skimo racing on groomed resort runs demands versatility and extreme fitness. Racing to cross the finish line first, each athlete in the 3-minute sprint up 230 feet and the 30-minute 460-foot relay is judged on speed and technical efficiency while navigating a set course with mandatory transitions.
Europe’s elite infantry units still use ski mountaineering for specialized warfare, patrolling and reconnaissance, and so military personnel compete in the now-thriving Alps’ skimo circuit. Troops from the Alpini (Italian Army), for example, are poised for success at the 2026 Winter Olympics.
“In Europe, almost all of the skimo teams are funded by their government’s military,” says Sarah Cookler, Gibson’s coach as head of sport for USA Skimo at the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics and founder and director of Silverfork Skimo, a club training young racers in Brighton, Utah. “Many wear a military badge on their jackets. Their athletes are sponsored, and their faces are on billboards. This is how they make a living year-round. We’re far from that [in the U.S.], but since the inclusion in the Olympics, there’s been a lot more visibility and hype around the sport.”
The future of the sport
Today, skimo has turned into one of the fastest growing winter sports in the world. This is no surprise when you consider how participation in off-piste skiing has increased in the U.S. and Europe by more than 25 percent in the past five years. Global sales of the alpine touring skis used in the sport are projected to increase 3.9 percent by 2032 to reach $1,844 million. Meanwhile, about 65 percent of ski resorts now offer some form of uphill access for skinning, up from 33 percent a decade ago.
“With the expansion of ski lifts from the 1930s that continues today, most skiers lost the incentive to ski uphill, but a growing number have rediscovered the appeal, helped by lighter and more sophisticated equipment, better technique and the expansion of backcountry glades that are being developed apart from ski resorts,” says Leich.
Cookler’s Silverfork Skimo club is one of more than a dozen U.S. skimo clubs, including such major players as the Boulder Skimo Club, the Colorado Mountain Club and the Park City Skimo Team. Since skimo was announced as an Olympic sport, clubs have been expanding their youth teams.
“Skimo success requires not only an extremely high maximum oxygen uptake but also technical skinning skills and downhill skiing prowess,” says Gibson. “I love the feeling of pushing my heart and lungs the way that skimo requires.”
Her Olympic training has involved practicing transitions—from skin to ski or from bootpack to skin, for example—along with backcountry skiing, running and resort skiing.
“Most fit athletes have the power output to go fast uphill at a high altitude, especially in such short races. They have to have the strength and endurance to ski aggressively downhill,” explains Cookler. “But skimo athletes are actually keeping their heart rate elevated for two and a half hours, transitioning from one modality to another by taking skis off and putting skis back on.”
That makes skimo arguably one of the most physically demanding endurance sports in the world. And soon, on Thursday and Saturday, the world will see how a professional all-around athlete and her veteran USA teammate size up to the European skimo frontrunners on the Stelvio slope in Bormio, Italy.
“I remember watching my parents compete in a local skimo race called Jackson Hole Mountain Resort’s Randonnée Rally”—one of the pioneering skimo events in the U.S.—"back when I was 4 or 5 years old,” says 26-year-old Gibson, who also skinned up the resort as a kid. “Skimo has been in my blood for a long time.”

articles.title: Archaeologists Unearthed a 2,200-Year-Old Bone. They Say It Could Be the First Direct Evidence of Hannibal's Legendary War Elephants
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Archaeologists Unearthed a 2,200-Year-Old Bone. They Say It Could Be the First Direct Evidence of Hannibal’s Legendary War Elephants
The Carthaginian general famously used elephants during the Punic...
articles.content:
Archaeologists Unearthed a 2,200-Year-Old Bone. They Say It Could Be the First Direct Evidence of Hannibal’s Legendary War Elephants
The Carthaginian general famously used elephants during the Punic Wars. But until now, archaeologists had never found skeletal remains linking the animals to the conflict
In 2019, archaeologists unearthed a strange bone alongside a trove of ancient catapult projectiles in Córdoba, Spain. According to a study published this month in the Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports, the bone may be from one of Hannibal’s war elephants, which the Carthaginian general used against Romans in the Second Punic War.
The bone “could prove to be a landmark,” lead author Rafael Martínez Sánchez, an archaeologist at the University of Cordoba, tells Live Science’s Tom Metcalfe. Until now, “there has been no direct archaeological testimony for the use of these animals.”
The dig was conducted ahead of construction for a medical facility on the Colina de los Quemados archaeological site. Archaeologists documented evidence of occupation at the site across hundreds of years beginning around the Late Bronze Age. However, their most intriguing discoveries date to the late Iron Age.
Along with stone artillery and coins, researchers unearthed a “short, cube-shaped bone,” measuring nearly four inches long. Eventually, they determined that the bone likely came from the right leg of an African or Asian elephant.
“It could belong to the period of the Punic Wars,” Martínez Sánchez told El Pais’ Vicente G. Olaya in 2023. “It could be the first of Hannibal’s elephants to be discovered. We can’t know for sure, but it was certainly a sizable beast.”
In the years since, researchers have continued studying the bone. Because it is poorly preserved, DNA and protein analyses have proved inconclusive. The species of elephant also remains unconfirmed. However, after radiocarbon testing, researchers were able to conclude that the elephant died between the fourth and third centuries B.C.E.
This timeline aligns with the Second Punic War, which occurred in the third century B.C.E. This conflict was part of the series of wars between Rome and Carthage, an ancient city in modern-day Tunisia.
Study co-author Agustín López Jiménez, an expert at the archaeology company Arqueobética, which excavated the site, told El Pais that researchers found the elephant foot bone beneath some collapsed adobe walls dating to around the third century B.C.E. According to the study, the same area revealed 12 three-pound stone balls, which were “unquestionably artillery projectiles for lithoboloi,” a kind of catapult.
Quick facts: War elephants in antiquity
- After facing war elephants in modern-day Pakistan in 326 B.C.E., Alexander the Great brought some of the impressive animals back to the Mediterranean.
- Around 280 B.C.E., the Greek king Pyrrhus used elephants to fight the Romans, which, as Pliny the Elder later wrote, “tread under foot whole companies, and crush the men in their armor.”
The Second Punic War began after Hannibal attacked Saguntum, a city on the Iberian Peninsula that had allied with Rome. When Carthage refused to withdraw, Rome declared war in 218 B.C.E.
Hannibal led his forces northeast from the Iberian Peninsula, aiming for the Alps. The general planned to enter Italy from the north and attack, forcing the Romans to defend their turf. Hannibal’s army reportedly included some 30,000 soldiers, 15,000 horses and 37 war elephants. The mountain march was brutal, and he lost many of the elephants along the way.
Despite the historical record, archaeologists had never found skeletal remains of elephants connected to the Punic Wars—until the Córdoba bone. The researchers say that this bone didn’t come from “one of the mythical specimens Hannibal took across the Alps.” However, Martínez Sánchez tells IFLScience’s Russell Moul, the discovery “is the first time an elephant bone remnant linked to that chronology has been found in Iberia and, to our knowledge, in Europe.”

The organic building is a great example of this. The structure was conceptualized by Gaetano Pesce in the mid 80s and open
